text: the following video is of laparoscopic selenectomy from government medical college srinagar post graduate department of surgery and critical care medicine in the short video that you are about to see We are demonstrating laparoscopic splenectomy.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the omentum in front of the spleen, identifying two distinct tissue layers. The harmonic scalpel provides both hemostatic and dissecting capabilities. An Enseal device is employed near the stomach to manage the short gastric vessels. The splenic artery is visible at the six o'clock position.

The surgeon uses the Enseal device to dissect the short gastric vessels away from the stomach, ensuring hemostasis. Attention is then turned to the splenic hilum, where the vasculature is dissected using endoshears. A vessel is identified, skeletonized, and ligated proximally with hemolock clips. The splenic artery, noted for its early branching pattern, is dissected with the harmonic scalpel, revealing the splenic vein and artery branches. The main branch of the splenic artery is dissected free with a right angle dissector, clipped, and then cut with harmonic shears.

The surgeon presses the spleen from the superior aspect to redirect blood flow back into circulation. The splenic vein is addressed to prevent congestion, followed by further dissection of the hilum towards the superior pole using endoshears. Adhesions on the posterior aspect of the spleen are dissected free, allowing the spleen to be lifted off the hilum.
The surgeon dissects adhesions near the superior pole of the spleen, freeing the spleen from surrounding tissue.
